narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 56.3% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, family background, and enrollment plans, focusing on patterns seen in past students without considering gender or race. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 grade decile of 1, which places you in the bottom tenth of your school that year—far below the typical 6th decile among students who went on to pass the bar. This low ranking stood out as the strongest signal to the model, suggesting challenges in sustaining strong performance later in your studies. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 35.5 pulled the prediction toward success, as it was close to the average of 37.3 seen in bar passers, showing solid reasoning ability. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.4 worked in your favor, exceeding the 3.24 average for those who passed and highlighting a strong foundation from your prior degree. Your lower-middle family income bracket, while less common among bar passers (where middle and upper-middle incomes dominate), played a smaller role overall, as did your plan to enroll full-time, which aligns with the vast majority of successful students. Compared to typical admitted applicants—who often have higher deciles in later years and slightly stronger LSATs—your profile showed promise in early academics and testing but was held back by that Year 3 dip. The model weighs these elements to prioritize students with the highest odds of bar success, and in your case, the Year 3 grades tipped the balance. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year deciles, perhaps through targeted tutoring or additional undergrad-level work to build momentum. You might also retake the LSAT to push it even higher, as small gains there could shift your profile significantly. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these gaps—reach out to admissions for advice on reapplying with updated credentials, and keep pursuing your goals with that full-time commitment. Your strengths are evident, and with focused improvements, a future application could look much brighter.
